content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento. CA 95814-2329 
David G. Lim, Councilmember 
City of San Mateo 
313 Midvale A venue 
San Mateo, CA 94403 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-12-175 
Dear Mr. Lim: 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
December 28, 2012 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Please note that our advice is based solely 
on the provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other 
conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest. Also, the Commission does 
not act as a finder of fact in providing advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 72.) 
QUESTION 
Do City staff members who work on a permitting issue for a property three miles from 
your house raise any conflict of interest issues when the same staff members are involved with 
permits for your property's improvement permits? 
CONCLUSION 
No. City staff performing their job duties does not implicate any financial interest of 
yours such that a conflict of interest would arise. 
FACTS 
You are a city councilmember with the city of San Mateo. You applied with the city for 
a permit to add on to your residence, which was granted in December 2012. Three miles from 
your property, there is a 7-Eleven® store that was recently detennined to be operating in a 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections I 8110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2. Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-12-175 
Page No. 2 
residential zone. In January 2013, there will be a hearing before the city council to determine 
whether this non-conforming use may continue. The same city staff who worked on the 
permitting for your home-addition project is also assigned to the 7-Eleven® permit issue. You 
are requesting information regarding whether the commonality of city staff on these two projects 
would prohibit you from participating in the permitting decisions during the January 2013 
hearings. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials "perform their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial 
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, Section 87100 
prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her 
official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial 
interest. The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether an 
individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. (Regulation 
18700(b)(l)-(8).) 
Steps 1 and 2: Are you a public official, making, participating, or influencing a governmental 
decision? 
As a city councilmember, you are a public official who will be called upon to make a 
governmental decision regarding the permitting of a 7-Eleven® store about three miles from 
your property. 
Steps 3 and 4 Do you have an economic interest under the Act and is it directly or indirectly 
involved in the governmental decision? 
Under the Act, real property "in which the public official has a direct or indirect 
investment worth $2,000 or more in fair market value" is an "economic interest." (Regulation 
18703.2(a).) We assume that you have at least a $2,000 interest in your property, making it an 
economic interest under the Act. 
In pertinent part, Regulation 18704.2(a) states that real property in which a public official 
has an economic interest is directly involved in a governmental decision if: 
"(l) The real property in which the official has an interest, or any 
part of that real property, is located within 500 feet of the 
boundaries (or the proposed boundaries) of the property which is 
the subject of the governmental decision." 
Your property would be presumed to be directly involved in the decision if it were within 
500 feet of the government decision. At three miles away, it is indirectly involved in the 
governmental decisions regarding the 7-Eleven® store. 


File No. A-12-175 
Page No. 3 
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material.financial effect on your 
economic interests? 
For indirectly involved real property, the financial effect of a governmental decision is 
presumed not to be material. This presumption may be rebutted by proof that there are specific 
circumstances regarding the governmental decision, its financial effect, and the nature of the real 
property in which the public official has an economic interest, which make it reasonably 
foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on the real property in which 
the public official has an interest. (Regulation 18705.2(b)(l).) You have not offered any such 
circumstances; therefore the presumption is not rebutted. The city staffers that are in common 
with your home renovation project and the 7-Eleven® project do not rebut the presumption. 
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his or 
her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable" that 
the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest need not be certain 
or even substantially likely to be "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere 
possibility. (Regulation 18706(a); In re Thomer (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) You have not 
presented any facts indicating that there may be specific circumstances making it reasonably 
foreseeable that the materiality presumption may be rebutted. Because it is presumed not to be 
material, it cannot be reasonably foreseeable that there will be a material financial effect unless 
the presumption is rebutted. Thus, you do not have a conflict of interest within the meaning of 
the Act with respect to your real property. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
HMR:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Zackery P. Morazzini 
General Counsel 
By: 
Heather M. Rowan 
Counsel, Legal Division

your house raise any conflict of interest issues when the same staff members are involved with 
permits for your property's improvement permits?

CONCLUSION: No. City staff performing their job duties does not implicate any financial interest of 
yours such that a conflict of interest would arise.
